titleStmt.title: Letter from James Finn to May Fay, 27 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Finn

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter is from a series of correspondence between James Finn and his fiancée Mary (May) Fay. The letter refers to May's impending visit and also to a wedding of a person known to the both of them.James Finn and Mary Fay became engaged in January 1916 and married in June. They were not to be married long as in 1919 he fell ill with the great flu epidemic, it led to heart damage and he died in 1922 as she was expecting their fourth child. She held on to his letters as her reminder of him. James Finn (1877 - 1922) was a civil servant, working in the National Health Service Commission on Mount Street. Family history says that he was advising on the restructuring of the civil service as part of setting up the new government in 1922 and that eminent figures in that project were visiting him in his sick bed so that they could consult with him. Neither May nor James were politically active, although like many in the Civil Service, learned Irish. One can sense their shifting sympathies in the letters.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
27 Jan, 1916. My darling girl, This is going to be a very short
note indeed. Glad to see from your letter today
that mumps are better and that we may
really hope to see you both on Saturday. If
you are coming by first train let me know
as I should like to meet you at station
shall be on tenderhooks until then. By the
way wasn't a bit hurt over Miss Gertie Kelly's
remarks about the dancers. I suppose you'll
see the marriage in Dublin next week if you
care to turn up at Church. Where is it to be
and is Father McNamee going to pull the bolt.
If they're looking for a cheap job they ought to go
to the Registry Office as John threatened he would.
Remember me to your mother and Mrs S. Your loving little man
James
If USED FOR TRANSMISSION TO ANY PLACE ABROAD TO WHICH THE 1d. RATE
OF LETTER POSTAGE DOES NOT APPLY, THE ADDITIONAL POSTAGE REQUIRED
SHOULD BE PREPARED BY MEANS OF ADHESIVE POSTAGE STAMPS. Upsidedown on image Miss Fay Togherstown Ballina Mullingar
